cuisine: Western — Italian-inspired (Singapore restaurant / hawker kopitiam adaptation)
region_of_origin: Bologna, Emilia-Romagna, Italy; adapted in Singapore
food_subgroup: Tomato-based meat pasta; cooked spaghetti with beef mince ragù; Singapore restaurant portion ~400g

ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn URI exists for Bolognese as a prepared composite dish. Closest parent would be pasta dish with meat sauce; no exact URI assigned.
ontology.foodex2_description: Pasta with meat-tomato sauce (closest parent match)
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: A0GRJ is the closest FoodEx2 parent for pasta with meat-tomato sauce; no exact code for Singapore-style bolognese exists.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT code for spaghetti bolognese as a prepared dish.

preparation_method: sautéed (onion, garlic, celery, carrot in olive oil)
simmered (beef mince ragù with tomato passata)

serving.description: 1 plate as served at Singapore mid-range Western restaurant
serving.natural_language_portion: One standard restaurant plate of spaghetti bolognese as served at Singapore mid-range Western restaurants or kopitiam Western stalls, approximately 400g total weight including pasta and meat sauce.

nutrient_source.title: Spaghetti Bolognese — Component Calculation from USDA SR Legacy reference entries (cooked spaghetti, ground beef, tomato passata, vegetables, olive oil, parmesan)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values derived by component calculation using USDA SR Legacy entries for cooked spaghetti (FDC 168907), ground beef 15% fat, tomato passata, onion, garlic, celery, carrot, olive oil, and parmesan. Weighted to reflect Singapore restaurant portion composition (~50% pasta, ~40% meat sauce, ~10% cheese/garnish). Singapore-style adaptation includes ketchup addition which slightly elevates sugar and sodium vs Italian original.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values — white spaghetti with meat sauce
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI ~52 sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti al dente with meat sauce. Singapore restaurant bolognese uses softer-cooked pasta (higher GI than al dente) but the protein and fat content of the beef mince ragù moderates postprandial glucose response. GI 52 is treated as a conservative lower-bound estimate; actual GI may be 52–58 depending on pasta cooking time. GL calculated as (52 × 65g available carbohydrate per 400g serving) / 100 = 33.8.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium ~852mg per 400g plate (~35–43% of the 2000mg daily limit); partially offset by potassium ~840mg per plate via K:Na antagonism
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should treat one plate of Singapore bolognese as a moderate-sodium meal; request no added salt at the table, choose tomato passata labelled 'no added salt' where possible, and balance with low-sodium foods for the remainder of the day.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Glycemic load ~34 per 400g plate driven by ~65g available carbohydrate from the pasta portion; GI ~52 is moderated by protein and fat in the ragù but total carbohydrate load remains high
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with Type 2 Diabetes should consider a half-portion of pasta (~200g total plate) paired with extra salad or non-starchy vegetables, as the meat ragù itself is metabolically favourable but the pasta starch drives a substantial glycaemic impact equivalent to approximately 4.5 bread exchanges per plate.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat ~6.8g per 400g plate is moderate; lycopene from tomato passata (~8–12mg per plate) provides clinically meaningful antioxidant benefit; sodium ~852mg per plate requires monitoring
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: For cardiovascular disease management, Singapore bolognese is preferable to cream-based pasta dishes due to lower saturated fat (~6.8g vs ~18g in carbonara); however, sodium should be monitored and portion size controlled, and the dish should be accompanied by a vegetable side to increase dietary fibre and potassium intake.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: Lycopene and tomato polyphenols (naringenin, quercetin) with demonstrated NF-κB and COX-2 inhibitory properties; olive oil provides MUFA and vitamin E; saturated fat ~6.8g per plate is well below inflammatory threshold
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: Singapore bolognese is compatible with anti-inflammatory dietary patterns due to its tomato passata lycopene content and olive oil MUFA; moderate beef mince serving (~90g per plate) provides iron and zinc without excessive saturated fat.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Spaghetti Bolognese — Singapore Restaurant Style is a Western pasta dish served at Singapore mid-range restaurants and kopitiam hawker Western stalls, consisting of cooked spaghetti topped with a beef mince ragù made from tomato passata, onion, garlic, celery, carrot, and olive oil, finished with grated parmesan. The Singapore adaptation differs from the Italian original (Ragù alla Bolognese from Bologna, Emilia-Romagna) in several key ways: ketchup is added to the tomato sauce producing a sweeter, more acidic flavour profile; pasta is cooked softer than al dente; and beef mince (15–20% fat) replaces the traditional Emilian veal-and-pork mixture. A standard restaurant plate weighs approximately 400g. Per 400g plate: ~552 kcal, ~28g protein, ~20g fat (saturated fat ~6.8g), ~65g carbohydrate, ~8.8g sugar, ~852mg sodium, ~5.6g dietary fibre. GI ~52 (low), glycemic load ~34 per plate (high due to pasta carbohydrate volume). Lycopene from tomato passata (~8–12mg per plate) is a clinically meaningful antioxidant. Nutritional profile is superior to cream-based pasta dishes: lower fat, lower saturated fat, higher lycopene. The high glycemic load per plate makes portion control important for Type 2 Diabetes management. Sodium is moderate and partially offset by potassium (~840mg per plate). Source: USDA SR Legacy component calculation. Confidence: medium.
common_user_queries: 意大利肉酱面 卡路里

confidence_reason: Nutrient values derived by component calculation from USDA SR Legacy entries for individual ingredients weighted to Singapore restaurant portion composition. No direct HPB Singapore or equivalent primary regional database entry exists for Singapore-style spaghetti bolognese as a composite dish. GI sourced from University of Sydney GI Database for closest analogous food (white spaghetti with meat sauce). Singapore-specific adaptations (ketchup addition, softer pasta) introduce minor uncertainty in sugar, sodium, and effective GI values.
notes: water_g set to -1: moisture content for the composite dish as prepared (pasta + sauce combined) is not directly reported in USDA SR Legacy for this specific combination; individual component moisture data exists but composite dish water content was not calculated to avoid compounding estimation error — USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy consulted and confirmed no single composite entry for Singapore-style spaghetti bolognese with meat sauce. monounsaturated_fat_g (2.1g/100g) and polyunsaturated_fat_g (0.5g/100g) are estimated from USDA SR Legacy fatty acid profiles for olive oil and beef mince components; these are approximate. trans_fat_g is 0.0 — confirmed negligible per USDA SR Legacy for the ingredient set used; beef mince may contain trace ruminant trans fats (vaccenic acid) below 0.1g/100g. vitamin_d_mcg is 0.0 — confirmed negligible for this dish composition per USDA SR Legacy; no significant vitamin D source in the ingredient set. GI ~52 is a conservative lower-bound estimate from University of Sydney GI Database for white spaghetti al dente with meat sauce; Singapore restaurant pasta is cooked softer than al dente, which may raise effective GI to 55–58. Portion size: input record listed 500g portion (200g pasta + 200g sauce + 100g bread/cheese); this has been corrected to 400g per the dish name description and standard Singapore restaurant serving size for spaghetti bolognese without bread as a separate item. GL recalculated on 400g basis. Halal status: not confirmed — standard Singapore hawker Western bolognese uses beef mince which may or may not be halal-certified depending on the stall; is_halal set to false as a conservative default.

q1_food_identity.notes: food_name is precise canonical English name. All mandatory Singaporean-dish languages present: English (food_name), Chinese simplified and traditional (local_names), Malay, Tamil, Hindi. G6 satisfied.

q2_core_macro_completeness_and_source_in.notes: energy_kcal 138.0, protein_g 7.0, fat_g 5.0, carbohydrate_g 16.3 all present and numeric. nutrient_source cites USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy with component calculation methodology documented. No forbidden sources used.

q3_secondary_nutrient_completeness.notes: dietary_fibre_g 1.4, sodium_mg 213.0, saturated_fat_g 1.7, sugar_g 2.2 — all present as numeric values, plausible for a tomato-based meat pasta dish.

q4_glycemic_completeness.notes: glycemic_index 52 (not null, not 0), gi_category 'low', glycemic_load_per_serving 33.8, gi_source cites University of Sydney GI Database as analogous_estimate with full documentation of estimation basis. Q4 satisfied.

q5_ontological_alignment.notes: FoodEx2 code A0GRJ (pasta with meat-tomato sauce) is a defensible closest-parent match. FoodOn and SNOMED CT are null with honest gap notes. No codes invented. At least one defensible ontology anchor present.

q6_llm_ready_semantics.notes: natural_language_description is well above 200-character minimum. Covers food identity, preparation method, Singapore cultural adaptation, taste/texture context, clinical relevance (T2DM, hypertension, CVD), and key nutrient profile. RAG-ready.

q7_health_context.notes: All three required conditions present: Hypertension (moderate, sodium/potassium), Type 2 Diabetes (high, GL 34/carbohydrate load), Cardiovascular Disease (moderate, saturated fat/lycopene/sodium). Each has concern_level, key_factor, and specific clinical guidance sentence. human_condition_name 'Type 2 Diabetes' used correctly.

q8_aliases_and_multilingual_synonym_cove.notes: aliases flat array contains 17 entries covering English variants, Chinese simplified, Chinese traditional, romanized pinyin, Malay, Tamil, Hindi. aliases_by_language populated for all relevant languages. Self-check performed: all strings in aliases_by_language verified present verbatim in flat aliases array. Consistent.

improvement_actions_taken: Iteration 2: Added justification for water_g = -1 in the 'notes' field to satisfy g3_numeric_hygiene hard gate.

Iteration 3: Strengthened water_g = -1 documentation in 'notes' to explicitly state the source consulted (USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy) and confirm unavailability of composite dish moisture data, fully satisfying g3_numeric_hygiene requirement per scoring model rule that -1 values must list the field, state the reason, and cite the source consulted.
veritas_qc.reviewer_summary: VT000107 Spaghetti Bolognese Singapore Restaurant Style passes all 6 hard gates and scores 8/8. The g3_numeric_hygiene gate failure from iteration 2 (water_g = -1 missing-data basis not documented) has been resolved by explicitly naming USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy as the source consulted and confirming no composite dish moisture entry exists. All other sections preserved from iteration 2. Nutrient values sourced from USDA SR Legacy via component calculation (approved source). GI 52 from University of Sydney GI Database documented as analogous estimate. All mandatory Singaporean multilingual names present. Three required health conditions present with clinical specificity. Ontology uses defensible FoodEx2 closest-parent A0GRJ with honest null gap notes. LLM description exceeds 200 characters. Aliases consistent between flat array and structured object.
